title: 2019–2020 Australian Black Summer Bushfires
location: Eastern Australia (New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland, South Australia)
source: Royal Commission into National Natural Disaster Arrangements (Commonwealth of Australia, 2020); Australian Bureau of Meteorology; CSIRO; NASA FIRMS; Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS)
text:
The 2019–2020 Australian bushfire season, known as the "Black Summer," burned from approximately June 2019 through March 2020 across southeastern Australia. It was the most destructive fire season in Australian recorded history by burned area, economic loss, biodiversity impact, and international atmospheric visibility.

An estimated 18.6 million ha burned across Australia during the season, with approximately 12.6 million ha in New South Wales alone. Over 3,000 homes were destroyed and at least 33 people died directly from the fires; hundreds more deaths were attributed to smoke inhalation across eastern cities. An estimated 3 billion animals were killed or displaced, representing one of the largest acute wildlife mortality events ever recorded.

Smoke from the Black Summer fires was detected by Copernicus CAMS satellites circling the globe and reaching South America. The fires generated multiple pyrocumulonimbus (pyroCb) events — fire-generated thunderstorms that injected smoke into the stratosphere, affecting global atmospheric chemistry.

The 2019–2020 season followed Australia's hottest and driest year on record (2019), with the Indian Ocean Dipole in a strongly positive phase reducing rainfall across southeastern Australia.

The Royal Commission into National Natural Disaster Arrangements (2020) identified systemic gaps in Commonwealth-state coordination, early warning systems, and the capacity to scale up firefighting resources. It recommended a permanent national aerial firefighting fleet and improved interoperability between state services.

The Black Summer is a benchmark event in global wildfire science, cited in IPCC AR6 as evidence of climate change increasing fire weather frequency and severity.

title: 2018 Camp Fire — Deadliest California Wildfire on Record
source: California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection (CAL FIRE) Camp Fire Incident Report; USGS; NASA FIRMS; Congressional Research Service; Paradise town official records
text:
The Camp Fire ignited on 8 November 2018 near Camp Creek Road in Butte County, California, driven by strong Diablo winds and critically low humidity following an exceptionally dry autumn. It spread rapidly downslope into the town of Paradise.

The fire destroyed approximately 18,804 structures and killed 85 people, making it the deadliest and most destructive wildfire in California history. The entire town of Paradise (population ~27,000) was effectively destroyed within hours of ignition. The total burned area was approximately 62,053 ha (153,336 acres).

Fire spread rates in Paradise exceeded the ability of residents to evacuate via limited road networks, creating mass traffic gridlock. Many survivors fled on foot or sheltered in place. Victims were found in their vehicles, homes, and yards.

CAL FIRE determined the cause was electrical transmission lines owned by Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E). PG&E subsequently pleaded guilty to 84 counts of involuntary manslaughter and filed for bankruptcy, reaching settlements totaling over $13.5 billion with survivors and affected local governments.

The Camp Fire highlighted the intersection of wildland-urban interface (WUI) expansion, utility infrastructure aging, extreme wind events, and prolonged drought as compounding risk factors. Post-fire satellite imagery and NASA FIRMS data showed the complete consumption of vegetated parcels surrounding Paradise, with dNBR maps confirming high-severity burn across the WUI zone.

The disaster accelerated California's vegetation management programs, utility infrastructure hardening requirements, and Public Safety Power Shutoff (PSPS) protocols.

title: Climate Change and Wildfire Risk in Greece
source: IPCC AR6 WGI Chapter 12 (2021); Turco et al. (2017) Nature Climate Change; Greek National Climate Change Adaptation Plan (2016); Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S) regional assessments; JRC PESETA IV report
text:
Greece and the broader eastern Mediterranean are classified as a climate change hotspot — one of the regions projected to experience the most severe warming and drying trends globally. Multiple lines of evidence indicate that fire weather conditions in Greece are becoming more frequent and more intense.

**Observed trends**: The eastern Mediterranean has warmed approximately 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels since 1900, roughly 20% faster than the global mean. Summer precipitation has declined in many regions. EFFIS data show a trend toward larger individual fire events and longer fire seasons in Greece since the 1980s, with several record-breaking seasons occurring after 2000 (2007, 2021, 2023).

**Projected changes under RCP 4.5 and 8.5**: JRC PESETA IV and Copernicus C3S assessments project that:
- The number of days with extreme fire weather (FWI above 30) in Greece will increase by 30–70% by mid-century under intermediate emissions scenarios
- The fire season length will extend by 3–6 weeks in southern Greece
- Drought frequency will increase, with multi-year drought sequences becoming more common

**Compound events**: The intersection of heat waves, droughts, and Etesian wind episodes — the combination that drove the 2023 Evros, 2021 Evia, and 2007 Peloponnese fires — is projected to become significantly more frequent. Multi-region simultaneous fires, which overwhelmed response capacity in 2023, are a particular concern.

**Land use interaction**: Climate-driven fire risk amplifies on landscapes shaped by rural depopulation and land abandonment. Fuel accumulation on abandoned agricultural terraces and former pastures throughout Greece has increased the potential for high-intensity fire spread when fire-weather thresholds are exceeded.

title: Copernicus Emergency Management Service — Rapid Mapping
source: Copernicus EMS documentation; ESA Sentinel-2 mission documentation; JRC Technical Reports; copernicus.eu/en/services/emergency-management
text:
The Copernicus Emergency Management Service (Copernicus EMS) is a component of the EU's Copernicus Earth Observation programme, operated by the Joint Research Centre (JRC) on behalf of the European Commission. Its Rapid Mapping component provides satellite-derived geospatial information within hours to days of a disaster event.

**Activation**: Copernicus EMS Rapid Mapping can be activated by EU member state civil protection authorities, EU institutions, and certain international organizations. Activations are catalogued with an EMSR code (e.g., EMSR681 for the 2023 Dadia fire). Each activation is publicly archived at the Copernicus EMS portal.

**Products**: For wildfire activations, standard products include:
- *Delineation product*: perimeter of the burned area derived from satellite imagery
- *Grading product*: burn severity map using dNBR or related spectral indices
- *Damage assessment*: structural damage to buildings and infrastructure

**Imagery sources**: Primary sources are Copernicus Sentinel-1 (SAR, cloud-penetrating) and Sentinel-2 (multispectral, 10m visible/NIR bands, 20m SWIR). Commercial very-high-resolution (VHR) imagery from Pleiades, SPOT, or WorldView is used for urban damage assessment.

**dNBR methodology**: Burn severity grading uses the differenced Normalized Burn Ratio: dNBR = pre-fire NBR − post-fire NBR, where NBR = (NIR − SWIR) / (NIR + SWIR). USGS burn severity thresholds are used: enhanced regrowth (<−0.10), unburned (−0.10 to 0.10), low severity (0.10–0.27), moderate-low (0.27–0.44), moderate-high (0.44–0.66), high severity (>0.66).

**Timeliness**: First emergency products are typically delivered within 12–48 hours of activation. Full grading maps follow within 3–7 days depending on cloud cover and satellite revisit intervals.

title: Dadia National Park — Ecology and Biodiversity
source: WWF Greece; BirdLife International; EU Natura 2000 site dataform GR1110006; Hellenic Ornithological Society; IUCN Red List
text:
Dadia National Park (formally Dadia–Lefkimi–Soufli Forest National Park) covers approximately 43,000 ha in the Evros regional unit of northeastern Greece, designated as Natura 2000 site GR1110006. The park is one of the most ecologically significant areas in Europe for large raptors and is recognized as an Important Bird Area (IBA) by BirdLife International.

The park's forests are dominated by a mosaic of sessile oak (Quercus petraea), Valonia oak (Q. ithaburensis ssp. macrolepis), black pine (Pinus nigra), and Calabrian pine (P. brutia). These old-growth oak-pine stands provide critical nesting and roosting habitat for species that require large, undisturbed forested landscapes.

The most globally significant feature of Dadia is its breeding colony of the Black Vulture (Aegypius monachus, Cinereous Vulture), classified as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. Dadia hosts one of the largest Black Vulture breeding populations in Europe, with approximately 30–40 active nests counted in recent surveys before the 2023 fire. The park also supports significant populations of White-tailed Eagle (Haliaeetus albicilla), Short-toed Eagle (Circaetus gallicus), Imperial Eagle (Aquila heliaca), and Long-legged Buzzard (Buteo rufinus).

The 2023 Dadia wildfire burned through extensive areas of the park's core zone, destroying primary forested habitat used by Black Vultures and other raptors for nesting. Post-fire surveys by the Hellenic Ornithological Society documented displacement of vulture colonies and loss of established nest trees. Recovery of old-growth oak forest at Dadia is expected to require 50–100 years, posing a severe long-term threat to the Black Vulture colony's viability.

title: EFFIS — European Forest Fire Information System
source: European Forest Fire Information System (EFFIS) at effis.jrc.ec.europa.eu; San-Miguel-Ayanz et al. (2012) 'Comprehensive Monitoring of Wildfires in Europe'; JRC Technical Reports series
text:
The European Forest Fire Information System (EFFIS) is operated by the Joint Research Centre (JRC) of the European Commission. It provides harmonized near-real-time and historical wildfire monitoring data for EU member states and participating countries in Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa.

**Fire danger forecasting**: EFFIS produces daily fire danger forecasts using the Canadian Fire Weather Index (FWI) system, computed from ECMWF ERA5 and operational meteorological data. Daily maps at ~10 km resolution are published publicly and used by national civil protection agencies.

**Burned area monitoring**: EFFIS uses MODIS (250 m, near-real-time) and Sentinel-2 (10–20 m, post-event) for burned area delineation. The EFFIS Fire Database records burned area statistics from 2000 onward for all EU and candidate countries.

**Key statistics (2000–2023)**:
- Annual average burned area in the EU: approximately 400,000–500,000 ha (excluding outlier years)
- Countries with highest recurring burned area: Portugal, Spain, Greece, Italy
- Largest single-country seasons: Portugal 2017 (~540,000 ha), Greece 2007 (~270,000 ha), Greece 2021 (~105,000 ha), Greece 2023 (~200,000+ ha total)
- The 2023 Dadia/Evros fire (~96,000 ha) was the largest individual fire ever recorded in the EU in the Copernicus/MODIS satellite monitoring era

**Fire Weather Index thresholds used by EFFIS**: Low <5.2, Moderate 5.2–11.2, High 11.2–21.3, Very High 21.3–38.0, Extreme >38.0. These differ from the unitless hot_dry_windy composite index used in operational agent outputs.

**Data access**: Historical EFFIS data, including burned area perimeters, are freely downloadable via the EFFIS Data Server. Current-season near-real-time detections are available via GWIS (Global Wildfire Information System).

title: EU Civil Protection Mechanism and rescEU Aerial Firefighting Reserve
source: European Commission DG ECHO; EU Decision 1313/2013/EU and amendments; rescEU programme documentation; European Parliament Research Service briefings
text:
The EU Civil Protection Mechanism (EUCPM), established under Decision No 1313/2013/EU and significantly strengthened by Regulation 2021/836, coordinates disaster response assistance among EU member states and participating states when national capacities are overwhelmed.

**Activation process**: When a disaster exceeds national response capacity, the affected country submits a request through the Emergency Response Coordination Centre (ERCC) in Brussels. The ERCC matches requests against registered national assets in the European Civil Protection Pool (ECPP) and alerts member states. Deploying states bear transport costs; the EU co-finances up to 75% of eligible costs for pre-committed pool assets.

**rescEU aerial firefighting fleet**: Following the 2017 and 2018 European fire seasons, the EU created the rescEU reserve — a strategic stock of assets owned or leased at EU level, managed by the Commission, and deployed on Commission decision rather than voluntary member state offer. The aerial firefighting component includes Canadair CL-415 amphibious scooper aircraft and helicopters. As of 2023, rescEU maintained aircraft stationed in France, Italy, Spain, Croatia, and Sweden.

**2023 deployment**: During the 2023 Greek fire season (Rhodes, Evros, Alexandroupolis), ERCC coordinated the largest peacetime civil protection operation in EU history at that point. Aircraft from France, Italy, Croatia, and Spain were deployed under both ECPP and rescEU frameworks. Ground firefighting teams from 10+ member states assisted.

**ERCC monitoring**: The ERCC operates 24/7 and uses the Copernicus Emergency Management Service (EMS) to provide near-real-time satellite mapping to support operational coordination during active deployments.

title: 2021 Evia Wildfire
source: Copernicus EMS activation EMSR517; EFFIS; EU Joint Research Centre; Greek Civil Protection; WWF Greece post-fire assessment 2021
text:
The northern Evia wildfire ignited on 3 August 2021 near Rovies and burned for approximately twelve days, becoming one of the most destructive wildfires in modern Greek history prior to the 2023 season.

EFFIS estimated the total burned area at approximately 50,500 ha, making it the largest single fire in the 2021 Greek fire season. The fire destroyed vast areas of fir forest (Abies cephalonica) and pine woodland on the slopes of northern Evia, habitats that take decades to regenerate.

Around 2,600 residents were evacuated from villages including Gouvès, Avgaria, Dafni, and Pefki. The fire destroyed approximately 150 homes and several agricultural buildings. One shepherd was killed.

The EU Civil Protection Mechanism was activated, with aerial firefighting assets deployed from multiple member states. Ground teams from France, Romania, and Ukraine assisted Greek firefighters.

Copernicus EMS activation EMSR517 delivered post-fire damage maps showing that over 50% of northern Evia's forested area was destroyed or severely damaged. The fire also burned within the Natura 2000 network site GR2420001, threatening protected fir forest ecosystems.

Post-fire assessments by WWF Greece documented severe erosion risk and loss of carbon stocks. The 2021 Evia fire became a symbol of Greece's vulnerability to increasingly severe fire seasons linked to climate change, prompting parliamentary debate and civil protection policy reviews.

title: 2023 Evros (Dadia) Wildfire — Largest Wildfire Ever Recorded in the EU
source: Copernicus EMS activation EMSR681; EU Joint Research Centre EFFIS; Greek Civil Protection press releases; WWF Greece post-fire reports
text:
The Dadia wildfire ignited on 19 August 2023 in the Evros regional unit of northeastern Greece, inside and adjacent to Dadia National Park (Natura 2000 site GR1110006). Driven by strong northerly Etesian winds and a preceding heat wave, the fire expanded rapidly and burned for approximately 16 days before being brought under control.

EFFIS (European Forest Fire Information System) estimated the total burned area at approximately 96,000 ha, making it the largest wildfire ever recorded in the European Union in the modern satellite monitoring era. The fire destroyed large portions of the park's old-growth oak-pine forests, which host one of Europe's last breeding colonies of the globally endangered Black Vulture (Aegypius monachus).

The Greek government declared a state of emergency in Evros. Several villages were evacuated and at least two fatalities were attributed to the fire. EU Civil Protection Mechanism assets — Canadair CL-415 aircraft from France, Italy, and Croatia — were deployed under the rescEU aerial firefighting reserve.

Copernicus EMS activation EMSR681 produced rapid mapping products using Sentinel-2 and Pleiades imagery. Post-fire dNBR analysis confirmed high-severity burn scars across much of the park's core zone. The fire occurred alongside several other major simultaneous fires across Greece (Rhodes, Alexandroupolis area), making the 2023 season the most destructive in Greek recorded history.

title: 2018 Mati (Attica) Wildfire — Deadliest European Wildfire in Modern History
source: Greek Coroner's Office; Copernicus EMS activation EMSR280; EU Joint Research Centre; Human Rights Watch report 2019; Greek Parliament inquiry committee
text:
The Mati wildfire ignited on 23 July 2018 in the coastal settlement of Mati, approximately 30 km east of Athens in the municipality of Marathonas, East Attica. The fire spread within minutes through densely built residential areas directly to the sea, trapping residents and vacationers.

The official death toll reached 102 confirmed fatalities, making it the deadliest wildfire in Europe in the modern era. Dozens of victims were found in groups near the shoreline, having been overtaken while attempting to reach the sea. Hundreds were injured and over a thousand structures were destroyed.

The fire burned approximately 1,000 ha of residential, forested, and scrubland area over a very short period of intense activity. Extreme fire behavior was attributed to a combination of strong, shifting Etesian winds, severe drought, and the dense unplanned construction that had developed over decades without firebreaks or cleared evacuation corridors.

Copernicus EMS activation EMSR280 provided rapid mapping products that documented the burn extent and structural damage.

Post-incident investigations revealed systemic failures: illegal construction in forested areas, absence of a village street plan making evacuation routes impassable, delayed emergency response, and breakdown of fire suppression coordination. Criminal prosecutions followed against municipal officials and firefighting commanders.

The Mati disaster prompted major reviews of Greek civil protection law, illegal construction enforcement, and urban-wildland interface planning regulations.

title: Mediterranean Fire Regime — Drivers and Characteristics
source: Pausas & Keeley (2009) Trends in Ecology & Evolution; Turco et al. (2017) Nature Climate Change; EFFIS Annual Fire Reports; San-Miguel-Ayanz et al. (2013) Forest Ecology and Management
text:
The Mediterranean climate zone (hot dry summers, mild wet winters) creates a predictable seasonal fire regime. The combination of summer drought, senescent vegetation, and persistent dry winds produces recurring high fire-danger periods typically lasting June through September in southern Europe.

**Key meteorological drivers**: Fire weather in the Mediterranean is primarily driven by three interacting factors:
1. *Temperature and drought*: summer maxima above 35–40°C accelerate fuel drying; multi-week dry spells reduce live and dead fuel moisture to critical levels
2. *Wind*: persistent regional winds — the Etesian (Meltemi) in the Aegean, the Tramontane and Mistral in France, the Ponente in Spain — promote rapid fire spread and suppress aerial firefighting
3. *Relative humidity*: values below 20–30% at critical thresholds for flashy fire behavior

**Etesian winds and Greece**: The Etesian (Meltemi) wind system dominates the Aegean from June through September, with northerly or north-easterly flows reaching sustained speeds of 40–60 km/h. The 2023 Dadia fire, the 2021 Evia fire, and the 2007 Peloponnese fires all occurred during Etesian episodes.

**Lightning ignition vs. human ignition**: In Mediterranean Europe, human ignition (accidental, arson, agricultural burning) accounts for over 90% of documented fire starts. Lightning ignition is more common in northern Mediterranean mountains and Iberia.

**Fire return intervals**: Natural or quasi-natural fire return intervals in Mediterranean pine and shrubland ecosystems are estimated at 15–50 years. Abandonment of rural land since the 1970s has dramatically increased fuel loads, reducing effective fire return intervals and increasing the potential for high-severity fire behavior when ignition occurs.

title: 2007 Peloponnese Wildfires — Deadliest Greek Fire Season Pre-2018
source: Greek Ministry of Environment; EFFIS; European Commission report on the 2007 Greek fires; WWF Greece; Reuters/AP contemporary coverage
text:
The summer of 2007 produced the most destructive fire season in Greece since systematic records began, centered primarily on the Peloponnese peninsula. A series of simultaneous fires ignited across Achaia, Ilia, Arcadia, and Messenia prefectures in late August 2007.

The largest single fire burned in the Ilia prefecture, destroying ancient olive groves, pine forests, and the landscape surrounding the ancient sanctuary of Olympia. The total burned area across the Peloponnese exceeded 170,000 ha, representing the vast majority of the national total of approximately 270,000 ha burned across Greece in 2007. These figures made 2007 the worst fire year in Greek recorded history until the 2023 season.

At least 65 people died in Peloponnese fires, primarily in the villages of Artemida, Grammatiko, Dafni, and Makistos in Ilia prefecture, often trapped in homes or while attempting to flee on rural roads. Total Greek fire fatalities in 2007 reached approximately 77.

The fires generated significant political controversy. Accusations of arson for illegal land development (land grabbers exploiting laws restricting construction on forested land), combined with government delays in deploying EU assistance, led to criminal investigations and the resignation of several officials.

EU Civil Protection Mechanism aerial assets were deployed. The 2007 fires led to lasting reforms in Greek civil protection law, including tougher anti-arson provisions and an extended 10-year ban on development of burned land.

title: 2017 Pedrógão Grande Wildfire — Deadliest Portuguese Wildfire on Record
source: Portuguese Technical Commission of Independent Evaluation (CTAI) report 2017; EU Joint Research Centre; EFFIS; Reuters; Comissão Técnica Independente report to Portuguese parliament
text:
The Pedrógão Grande wildfire ignited on 17 June 2017 in the municipality of Pedrógão Grande in central Portugal, approximately 200 km northeast of Lisbon. The fire spread rapidly through eucalyptus and pine plantations under extreme heat, low humidity, and strong winds associated with a dry thunderstorm.

The fire killed 66 people on 17–18 June alone, predominantly on the N236 road between Figueiró dos Vinhos and Castanheira de Pêra where motorists were overtaken by fire while attempting to evacuate. A subsequent related fire complex in October 2017 (Oliveira do Hospital) killed an additional 45 people, bringing the combined 2017 toll to over 110 deaths — the worst wildfire disaster in Portuguese history.

The June fire burned approximately 44,500 ha. A subsequent independent technical evaluation (CTAI) criticized the fire suppression response, the flammability of eucalyptus and pine monocultures dominating the Portuguese forest landscape, inadequate road networks in rural areas, and failures in the warning systems and civil protection coordination.

The disaster prompted major policy debates in Portugal about eucalyptus plantation management, mixed-species afforestation requirements, and rural land abandonment (with consequent fuel accumulation). It also influenced EU-level discussions about forest management and wildfire risk under climate change.

The Pedrógão fire is frequently cited as a reference event in European wildfire risk assessments for its demonstration of how fire behavior in eucalyptus/pine stands can produce rapid rates of spread with lethal consequences.

title: Post-Fire Vegetation Recovery in Mediterranean Ecosystems
source: European Journal of Forest Research; EFFIS Post-Fire Assessment Guidelines; ESA Copernicus Land Service; Pausas & Keeley (2009) Trends in Ecology & Evolution; Moreira et al. (2020) Annual Review of Ecology
text:
Mediterranean ecosystems have co-evolved with fire over millennia, and many plant communities exhibit specific fire-adaptation traits. However, the rate and quality of recovery depend heavily on fire severity, post-fire climate conditions, and the interval since the previous fire.

**Resprouting and seeding strategies**: Dominant recovery mechanisms in Mediterranean shrublands (maquis, phrygana, garrigue) are resprouting from protected root crowns or lignotubers (Cistus, Quercus coccifera, Arbutus unedo) and obligate seeding triggered by heat or smoke (Cistus spp., Pinus brutia). Recovery of shrub cover can begin within weeks post-fire and reach 50–70% canopy closure within 3–5 years under average rainfall conditions.

**Forest recovery timelines**: Closed-canopy pine forests (Pinus halepensis, P. brutia) can regenerate from serotinous or semi-serotinous cones within 2–5 years if seed sources survived at fire margins. Fir forests (Abies cephalonica) on Greek mountains recover more slowly — 20–40 years for closed canopy. Old-growth oak woodlands (Quercus petraea, Q. pubescens) may require 50–100 years to restore structural complexity and associated biodiversity.

**dNBR as recovery predictor**: High-severity burn scars (dNBR > 0.44) are associated with significantly slower recovery trajectories due to near-complete loss of soil seed banks, root systems, and organic matter. Time-series NBR analysis (annual Sentinel-2 composites) is used by Copernicus to track recovery trajectories at landscape scale.

**Erosion risk**: The first 1–3 post-fire winters carry high erosion and flash flood risk, particularly on steep slopes. Post-fire stabilization interventions (straw mulch, contour felling, seeding) are recommended in ESA and EU civil protection guidelines for slopes exceeding 30%.

**Climate feedback**: Increased fire frequency under climate change may push some Mediterranean communities below minimum inter-fire interval for recovery, leading to type conversion from forest to persistent shrubland or grassland.

title: 2023 Rhodes Wildfire
source: EU Copernicus EMS activation EMSR683; Greek Civil Protection; EFFIS; BBC and Reuters news coverage July 2023
text:
The Rhodes wildfire ignited on 18 July 2023 near Kiotari on the southeastern coast of Rhodes island and spread rapidly northward driven by strong winds and drought conditions. The fire burned for approximately ten days before being contained.

EFFIS estimated the burned area at roughly 12,500 ha, predominantly consisting of pine forest, maquis scrubland, and rural land. The fire was among the largest ever recorded on the island.

The human impact was significant: Greek Civil Protection authorities ordered the evacuation of at least 19,000 residents and tourists from coastal resorts and villages including Kiotari, Gennadi, Pefki, and Lindos-area communities. It was described at the time as the largest evacuation in Greek history, with emergency ferries used to move evacuees from beaches.

EU Civil Protection Mechanism assets were deployed via the rescEU aerial firefighting reserve, with Canadair CL-415 aircraft from Croatia, France, Spain, and Italy assisting Greek Air Force aircraft. Ground firefighting teams from several EU member states were also dispatched.

Copernicus EMS activation EMSR683 produced rapid mapping products and burn severity analysis using Sentinel-2 imagery. Post-fire dNBR maps confirmed high-severity burn scars across interior forested zones.

The fire occurred during a prolonged heat wave affecting most of Greece, with temperatures exceeding 40°C in several regions. Rhodes 2023 was part of a historically destructive fire season that also included the Evros/Dadia fire (96,000 ha) and fires near Alexandroupolis, making 2023 the most damaging season in Greek recorded history.
